questions.specification: Reverse engineering helps establish:
Understanding a product better allows designers to:
Reverse engineering can help determine:
Identifying product weaknesses can lead to:

Which of the following would NOT appear in a SWOT analysis of a new laptop?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXf0BXvsREElJA-eDOqCuL-Z2byk767FpdMEzcoF8kn138fN5Czq0YCVdxrNir9OAVMcReMyMZAZrWh93-LioqAT-Q1HsJsIbEtdCrAEpQOgEYZxenDgdZ8xj8gf1vX5xhM_2VmMAw?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.markscheme: This is an internal weakness directly related to the product's performance.
This is an external threat that affects the market competitiveness of the laptop.

While a weakness, factory layout inefficiency is an operational issue for the firm's production facilities. In a SWOT analysis specifically focusing on a new product (the laptop), factors typically relate to the product's features, target market, and direct competition rather than general corporate production logistics.
This is an external opportunity to increase sales and market share for the laptop.

questions.specification: How does product analysis help guide future iterations of a product?

options.content: It provides feedback to improve design decisions
options.markscheme: Product analysis involves evaluating an existing product's performance, usability, and features. The resulting feedback identifies specific areas for improvement, which designers use to make informed decisions for subsequent versions of the product.
Predicting competitor revenue is typically a function of market research and financial analysis rather than the technical or user-focused analysis of a specific product's design.

Government policy changes are identified through environmental scanning (e.g., PESTLE analysis), which focuses on external macro-environmental factors rather than the evaluation of a product's design and function.

While product analysis might influence packaging requirements, its primary role in guiding future iterations is to provide broad feedback for overall design improvement rather than just finalizing specific details like packaging size.

questions.specification:
A team dismantles a smartphone to study its internal design and materials. What process are they performing?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/4e02fd74-9a1a-46cb-86eb-309832f22953.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Reverse engineering is the process of deconstructing a product to understand its design, materials, and functional components. This is often done to analyze competitors' products or to improve existing designs.

questions.specification: A design team is developing a modular wall-mounted storage system for urban apartments. The aim is to maximize adaptability, minimize visual clutter, and cater to varied user needs within compact living spaces.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a modular wall-mounted shelving unit with detachable components.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/u1q7GTfjYDA-vvW2kcOeY)

Outline one reason why the modular nature of this storage system is suitable for users living in small apartments.
parts.markscheme:
• Allows users to reconfigure based on available space and needs <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 max] for a correct point related to space-efficiency or adaptability.*

parts.content: Identify two different contexts in which this storage system might be used, apart from home storage.
parts.markscheme:
• Retail displays <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Office workstation organization <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [2 max] for naming two distinct, plausible usage contexts.*

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a user interacting with the wall-mounted unit, customizing its layout using magnetic brackets.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ZCSQtiKA3a8G6Uxgj39Oe)

Explain how user-centered design principles are reflected in the configuration system shown.
parts.markscheme:
• Enables easy repositioning of components without tools <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Responds to individual user preferences and ergonomic needs <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [2 max] for clear links to user control and usability.*

parts.content: Describe two functional requirements that this wall-mounted system must fulfill for safety and performance.
parts.markscheme:
• Must securely support the weight of stored items <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Must remain stable when users add/remove modules <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [2 max] for valid and distinct functional requirements.*

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a technical drawing of the mounting interface and materials used in the shelf unit.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/qZa9IWGP9LSkiHC2LpXQk)

Justify the designer’s choice of using a metal bracket and hardwood platform for this application.
parts.markscheme:
• Metal provides structural strength for mounting and load-bearing <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Hardwood offers durability and visual warmth for domestic settings <marks>1 mark</marks>

• The combination ensures long-term safety and aesthetic appeal <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [3 max] for well-reasoned justification combining material properties and design context.*

parts.content: Evaluate the extent to which this system meets the diverse functional and aesthetic expectations of urban users.
parts.markscheme:
• Highly functional due to its adaptability, modularity, and compact form <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Aesthetic design is minimalist, blending with varied interior styles <marks>1 mark</marks>

• May not suit all users due to potential installation complexity or load limitations <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Trade-off exists between visual minimalism and storage capacity <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Overall effectiveness depends on specific user preferences and intended use <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Good fit for urban lifestyles prioritizing flexibility and space-efficiency <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [6 max] for a balanced evaluation supported by evidence from the figure.*

questions.specification: Gathering feedback from stakeholders helps to:

parts.content: Outline how reverse engineering supports product analysis.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** internal components and their arrangement through product disassembly. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Analyze** the materials and manufacturing techniques used in the product's construction. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Determine** potential areas for performance enhancements or cost reductions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Evaluate** how effectively the product design meets specific user requirements. <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] for each point outlined, up to [4].</note>

questions.specification: Which component of a SWOT analysis focuses on identifying market opportunities?

options.markscheme: Opportunities (O) in a SWOT analysis represent external factors that a business can exploit to its advantage, such as market growth, technological advancements, or changes in consumer behavior.

questions.specification:
A user complains about the defects with their coffee machine. What concept does this relate to?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/003d98b2-97c4-4d34-9354-9b3a5cc8a05e.jpeg)

A design team is evaluating an electric kettle for its usability, material choice, and potential for product improvement. They have gathered product views, common user complaints, and disassembly observations.

parts.content:
**Figure 1**: A front and side view of a basic electric kettle with visible water level indicator and plastic handle.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/gIvczoQAcr7SKpQy45czv)

State one primary function of the electric kettle.
parts.markscheme: - To boil water (heat water to boiling temperature). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline two features that support user safety when operating the kettle.
parts.markscheme:
- The plastic handle stays cool to the touch during heating. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The automatic shut-off feature prevents overheating or boiling dry. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2**: A photo of an electric kettle with the lid open, showing internal scale build-up and the heating element.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/Lm2MnKb1Zt4q0R6692jIC)

Explain how this image could inform product improvements related to maintenance.
parts.markscheme:
- Scale build-up indicates the need for easier cleaning or descaling solutions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Exposed heating element may suggest a redesign using concealed base heaters. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reveals opportunity to use scale-resistant materials like stainless steel. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3**: A simple exploded view diagram of the kettle showing base, heating plate, switch, body, and lid.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/jR63hJTPHDEYxfGa9t3Mq)

Describe one reason why exploded diagrams are useful for designers evaluating a product.
parts.markscheme:
- Helps identify how components are connected for ease of repair or replacement. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allows assessment of material usage and manufacturing complexity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how user testing might reveal ergonomic design issues in this product.
parts.markscheme:
- Users may find the lid hard to open, especially when hot. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Water level markers could be hard to see during filling. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Handle grip might be uncomfortable for users with smaller hands. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Weight when full may cause wrist strain, highlighting balance issues. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the usefulness of combining material analysis and stakeholder feedback when redesigning this kettle.
parts.markscheme:
- Stakeholders (e.g., users, retailers) give feedback on comfort, noise, and appearance. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Material analysis reveals which components degrade quickly or retain heat dangerously. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Designers can choose materials that better meet functional and emotional needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Combines real-world experiences with lab-tested performance data. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports informed decisions for improving sustainability and durability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enhances both product reliability and market competitiveness. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
What aspect of product analysis is best illustrated by this exploded diagram of a consumer electronic device?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdk-QocRpOlUAXP0wEwN1Xq1-7HeZKUqha6YYF_j6J8JtszOOC6_zg_SpASyXx9S1itwkWVNGpO0ajnhPz4P3yZiQMZSDc-OP_xUUOiv3N8NnbpF6iBbdY8T5Jsze9GSR28Qn1k?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Aesthetic surface finishes used in design
Analysis of product assembly and disassembly

questions.specification: What does the "T" in SWOT stand for?

options.markscheme: Incorrect. While trends are analyzed within a SWOT framework (often as part of opportunities or threats), the letter T specifically stands for Threats.
Incorrect. SWOT analysis does not include a 'Timeframe' component in its core acronym.
Correct. SWOT is an acronym for Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats.
Incorrect. Technology is an external factor often evaluated in the 'T' of PESTLE analysis, but in SWOT, the T stands for Threats.

questions.specification: A startup company is designing a new line of collapsible picnic tables for families who enjoy outdoor activities. The product must cater to portability, ease of use, and visual appeal while being robust enough for repeated outdoor use.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a collapsible picnic table with attached benches unfolded.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/fRbtXFehlvYfFhCwrVO0e)

State one functional requirement of this picnic table in its collapsed state.
parts.markscheme: - Must be compact enough for easy transportation or storage <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify two factors that would influence the ergonomic suitability of the table when unfolded.
parts.markscheme:
- Bench height in relation to table surface <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ease of access to seating for various age groups <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a family of four using the picnic table at a park.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/bZu5p-oHV4gshO6DQUTUH)

Explain one way the context of use in Figure 2 influences the design of the picnic table.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies a relevant contextual factor (e.g. outdoor park use, uneven ground, exposure to rain/UV, mud/grass) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains a linked design response (e.g. weather-resistant materials/finishes, corrosion resistance, non-slip feet, wider leg stance for stability) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe two aesthetic considerations that must be taken into account when designing for family leisure products.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of bright or neutral colors to appeal to a wide demographic <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Smooth edges and clean shapes to enhance visual friendliness and safety <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a technical diagram of the table’s locking hinge and leg reinforcement system.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/Wl4BnUDrt2BISN3-DmtIZ)

Discuss three design features in the hinge mechanism that support user safety and functional performance.
parts.markscheme:
- Locking mechanism prevents unintended collapse during use <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reinforced joints improve load-bearing capacity and longevity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Smooth hinge action enhances ease of setup and takedown <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate how well the collapsible picnic table addresses the needs of both portability and durability for outdoor leisure users.
parts.markscheme:
- Foldable design allows convenient storage and transport <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Materials like aluminum or treated hardwood withstand environmental exposure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Trade-offs may exist between low weight and long-term strength <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compact design may limit comfort or size for larger groups <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Overall, strikes a balance between user-friendly setup and ruggedness for casual outdoor use <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Successful in targeting casual family users, but may fall short for heavy-duty or commercial use <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: What does a SWOT analysis help identify?
A designer is evaluating a cordless drill as part of a product development review. They are considering aspects such as performance, aesthetics, materials, and environmental impact.

parts.content:
**Figure 1**: A side view of a basic cordless drill, with visible trigger, handle, battery, and drill head.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/E7PrlcZqaW3o4X_XR1eD_)

State one primary function of the cordless drill.
parts.markscheme:
• To drive screws or drill holes into various materials using rotational force. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 max] for clearly identifying the product's core mechanical function.*

parts.content: Outline two ways the visual design supports the drill’s usability.
parts.markscheme:
• The ergonomic handle shape supports a firm and natural grip. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• The bright color improves visibility on construction sites. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [2 max] for visual or form-based elements that enhance use.*

parts.content:
**Figure 2**: A close-up image of a hand holding the drill in use, focusing on user posture and grip.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/NHrA13s1hu3ak8KjOvZIA)

Explain one ergonomic feature of the drill visible in this image.
parts.markscheme:
• The trigger placement allows for easy one-finger control without adjusting grip. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• The rubberized surface improves comfort and reduces slippage. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [2 max] for identifying and justifying ergonomic details based on user interaction.*

parts.content: Describe one way user-centered design is reflected in this product.
parts.markscheme:
• The lightweight design ensures users can operate the drill with less fatigue. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Interchangeable battery systems meet user demand for flexible charging. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [2 max] for examples that link product decisions to user needs or preferences.*

parts.content:
**Figure 3**: A simple teardown view of the cordless drill showing internal components.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/rXA6q_0JHAQJCCpogJ8Lv)

Discuss how cost considerations may have influenced material selection in this product.
parts.markscheme:
• The casing is made of injection-molded plastic to minimize manufacturing costs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Lithium-ion batteries balance cost with performance and durability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Simplified component layout reduces assembly time and labor costs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Replacing metal gears with plastic parts in some models cuts cost but reduces lifespan. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [4 max] for linking specific material or structural choices to cost-saving motives.*

parts.content: Evaluate the environmental impact of this product throughout its life cycle.
parts.markscheme:
• Battery disposal poses hazards due to lithium and heavy metals. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Plastic casing may be recyclable but not biodegradable. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Long product lifespan can reduce overall material waste. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Rechargeable batteries reduce reliance on disposable batteries. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Disassembly for recycling is possible but often neglected due to design complexity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Production and distribution involve carbon emissions from materials and logistics. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [6 max] for a critical and balanced evaluation across different stages of the life cycle.*

questions.specification: Which tool helps designers identify opportunities?
Constructive discontent is typically triggered by:
Stakeholder input is essential to:

options.content: Evaluate a product from multiple perspectives

parts.content: Evaluate the benefits and limitations of using SWOT analysis to guide product redesign.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** areas where a product performs well (strengths). <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Highlight** flaws that may reduce market competitiveness (weaknesses). <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Support** strategic planning by identifying external opportunities. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Encourage** risk management through awareness of external threats. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Acknowledge** that SWOT may lack depth if not supported by user data. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Recognize** that it relies on subjective interpretation without empirical testing. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Note** that it is useful for quick insights but is not comprehensive. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Suggest** its use alongside other evaluation tools for a more complete analysis. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Conclude** that it provides a broad but initial framework for redesign. <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] per balanced benefit/limitation with evaluation, up to [9 max].</note>

questions.specification:
## Question
What is tested in the early stages of product analysis?

A design team is developing a new set of headphones and has conducted benchmarking on two existing products in the market. The following table summarizes the findings from their research:

| Feature | Product A (Studio Pro) | Product B (Travel Lite) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Material** | Stainless steel and genuine leather | Recycled ABS plastic and synthetic foam |
| **Usability** | Wired connection only; fixed headband | Wireless Bluetooth; foldable design |
| **Durability** | Rated for 10+ years; heavy-duty hinges | Rated for 2 years; plastic hinges prone to cracking |
| **Ergonomics** | Heavy ($450\text{ g}$); high clamping force | Lightweight ($180\text{ g}$); soft memory foam cups |
| **User Feedback** | "Excellent sound and indestructible, but hurts after an hour." | "Very comfortable and convenient for travel, but feels fragile." |
| **Benchmarking** | Top-tier professional audio standard | Mid-range consumer convenience standard |

parts.content: Compare the findings for Product A and Product B to identify six specific improvement opportunities for the design of a new headphone product.
parts.markscheme:
- **Material/Durability**: Contrast Product A's steel construction with Product B's plastic hinges; a new design could use metal reinforcements at stress points to improve the lifespan beyond Product B's 2-year rating. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Ergonomics/Weight**: Compare the weight of Product A ($450\text{ g}$) and Product B ($180\text{ g}$); an improvement opportunity exists to use lightweight alloys to provide Product A's strength without the discomfort caused by its mass. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Usability/Connectivity**: Identify that Product B offers wireless convenience while Product A is wired; the new design should incorporate wireless technology to meet consumer travel standards while maintaining audio quality. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **User Comfort**: Analyze the feedback regarding clamping force and foam type; using memory foam (from Product B) in a more adjustable headband (improved from Product A) would allow for longer periods of use without pain. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Portability**: Contrast the fixed design of Product A with the foldable design of Product B; an improved professional product should include a folding mechanism for better storage without sacrificing the heavy-duty nature of the hinges. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Market Positioning**: Utilize benchmarking data to suggest a hybrid product; there is an opportunity to create a "Professional-Travel" category that combines the indestructible build of Product A with the comfort and portability of Product B. <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] for each valid comparison that clearly identifies a specific improvement opportunity based on the provided data.</note>

questions.specification: What does constructive discontent aim to identify?

options.content: Areas where user needs aren't being met

parts.content: Discuss how testing data can be used to evaluate function and performance.
parts.markscheme:
- Testing data reveals product limitations under various conditions, allowing designers to identify where the product may fail or perform poorly. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It confirms whether the product meets expected performance standards by providing quantitative data to compare against the initial design specification. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Analyzing data from repeated tests helps assess consistency and reliability, ensuring the product functions the same way every time. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The data provides a basis for material or component optimization, indicating where improvements can be made to enhance performance or efficiency. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It enhances decision-making in iterative redesign by providing empirical evidence to justify specific design changes or refinements. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Testing data supports compliance with regulatory or safety requirements, ensuring the product meets legal and safety standards for consumer use. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] per well-explained use of testing data, up to [6 max].</note>

questions.specification: What is the primary purpose of product analysis?

options.content: To estimate the cost of raw materials
To identify strengths, weaknesses, and opportunities for improvement
To determine the product's branding potential

questions.specification: What does reverse engineering generate?

parts.content: Identify two key stakeholders involved in product evaluation.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify end-users as a key stakeholder. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify engineers or manufacturers as a key stakeholder. <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] for each correctly identified stakeholder, up to [2 max]. Other valid stakeholders may include designers, clients, or retailers.</note>

questions.specification:
## Question
How does comparing competing products support product improvement?

![Diagram showing the three main outputs of comparing competing products: Identify Strengths and Weaknesses, Establish Standards, and Identify Opportunities.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/c3d47a67-f133-41b5-a6ff-9953b2eab2a6.jpeg)

parts.content: Explain the purpose of conducting product analysis in the design process.
parts.markscheme:
- Helps identify the strengths and weaknesses of a product <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides insights for future improvements or redesign <marks>1 mark</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each valid purpose of product analysis, up to [2 marks].</note>

questions.specification: Which group should be consulted during the product analysis process?

parts.content: Analyse how stakeholder feedback influences the development of a product.
parts.markscheme:
- Users may suggest changes that improve comfort or usability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Manufacturers can advise on feasibility and cost implications. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Retailers may highlight packaging or shelf appeal issues.<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Engineers might provide insight into technical performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Feedback can identify overlooked user needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Collaborative evaluation improves the likelihood of market success. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each clearly linked stakeholder insight, up to 6 marks.*

questions.specification:
In a SWOT analysis, what would “threats” typically include?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXf0BXvsREElJA-eDOqCuL-Z2byk767FpdMEzcoF8kn138fN5Czq0YCVdxrNir9OAVMcReMyMZAZrWh93-LioqAT-Q1HsJsIbEtdCrAEpQOgEYZxenDgdZ8xj8gf1vX5xhM_2VmMAw?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

Which activity helps designers identify how well a product performs for its intended users?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdIExnqlyytQWfDvrrjR5DTg7ebsWh74_hqRnPMlPDYhI_PQkPn3Yr--tS0PPfUQe8-r9WS8rxTJpneyz_CSncla6_tl0DmE_zkJfOchNkSZIbnOV8DMERo3OwNxXTtvwo81pJmbw?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: To explore environmental impact in recycling
options.markscheme: This relates to life cycle assessment/sustainability analysis, not testing performance with intended users.

options.content: To benchmark features and performance of competitors
options.markscheme: Benchmarking compares a product against competitors; it does not directly assess performance with intended users using the product.

options.content: To identify user needs based on social trends
options.markscheme: This is user research/needs identification (e.g., trend analysis), not evaluating product performance through use.

options.content: To conduct a user trial with intended users in normal use conditions
options.markscheme: A user trial involves intended users testing the product in the situation it would normally be used in to evaluate performance/usability.

questions.specification: Product evaluation helps identify:
Why is product analysis important for designers?

options.content: To determine the market retail price

questions.specification: Reverse engineering is useful for examining a product’s:

A design team is reviewing a recently launched electric kettle model to determine areas for future product improvement. They are assessing its performance, identifying stakeholder feedback, and evaluating how well the product meets user needs.

parts.content:
**Figure 1**: A side view of a modern electric kettle placed on a kitchen countertop. The kettle has a clear water-level indicator, a plastic handle, and a metal heating base.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/Ud32rEaYNjSAFxczBe6X_)

Identify one likely stakeholder, apart from the end user, who may be involved in the product analysis process of the kettle.
parts.markscheme:
• Manufacturer or supplier <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1 max] for any relevant stakeholder other than the end user.*

parts.content: Explain one purpose of conducting product analysis on the kettle shown.
parts.markscheme:
• To assess usability and safety for consumers <marks>1 mark</marks>

• To identify any areas of weakness in its function or design <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [2 max] for a clear and relevant explanation of why product analysis would be done.*

parts.content:
**Figure 2**: A diagram showing a SWOT table for the electric kettle with headings but no content filled in.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/rSNPEInA5as-o0NclsQJA)

Complete the SWOT table by suggesting one possible entry for each of the four sections based on the kettle product.
parts.markscheme:
• Strength – fast boiling time <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Weakness – short power cord <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Opportunity – integrate smartphone connectivity <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Threat – newer models from competitors with better features <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid SWOT point linked to the kettle.*

parts.content:
**Figure 3**: A close-up image of the kettle's lid hinge and spout filter, both showing signs of wear after several uses.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/RA2ObvdorG8_SbZuSONEr)

Using figure 3, describe one piece of data that could be obtained through reverse engineering and how it might inform product development.
parts.markscheme:
• Data on material fatigue at the lid hinge <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Can be used to identify a weakness in material choice or design <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Helps inform future improvements to increase product lifespan <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [3 max] for identifying relevant data, explaining its implication, and linking to improvement.*

parts.content: Explain how constructive discontent could be used to identify areas where the kettle does not meet user expectations.
parts.markscheme:
• Involves gathering feedback about recurring frustrations <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Users may complain about noise levels or lid opening issues <marks>1 mark</marks>

• These problems highlight unmet needs <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Can drive designers to focus improvements on user pain points <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [4 max] for clear application of constructive discontent to the product.*

parts.content: Discuss how data gathered from stakeholders and testing contributes to a better understanding of the product and opportunities for redesign.
parts.markscheme:
• Feedback reveals what aspects users appreciate or dislike <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Performance testing uncovers failure points (e.g., leaking base) <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Engineers may suggest material changes or design alterations <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Sales data may indicate market reception or usability concerns <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Allows the design team to prioritize improvements based on evidence <marks>1 mark</marks>

• Leads to better-targeted, user-focused product redesigns <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per well-explained point up to [6 max], ensuring a balance of stakeholder and test data.*

questions.specification:
What is the primary reason designers conduct product analysis during development?

questions.specification: When is constructive discontent especially useful?

options.content: When needs are unmet by current designs
